replies.author: @ahemagain4309
replies.text: With a dozen random pieces of optical calcite & sometimes selenite, if you test in a dark space with a blacklight flashlight you'll find a few that "hold"(?) the light very briefly, 1-2 secs at most. It's a very odd effect. Perhaps contamination with some phosphorescent material like zinc sulfide w/explain it. Seems like something else. Same exact color as whatever light induces it. I know gemologists who carry a light for this purpose. Never found a good explanation.

author: @dedlunch
text: I wonder if you could make this into a fine powder and add a small percentage to you transparent flexible solar panel? Might boost efficiency by capturing and distributing more light to the cell....hmmmm....very interesting:)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's probably dependent on the crystal structure so making it too small a powder might well destroy it - slow growth of nanocrystals might be good

author: @stabarinde
text: Hi Rob, what's the name of the crystal? BisthioEurea Zinc Formate? Can you write down the step by step method and quantities for synthesising it? I might have an application of such a crystalline substance...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's it - it's bisthiourea zinc formate - i'm not going to write out a step method mate - i have done the vid, linked to the synthesis vid and that's it - writing stuff out takes ages and i just don't like doing it - all the info you need is in the vid

replies.author: @stabarinde
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith yeah, it's just hard to hear the quantities in the synthesis vid. I'll start buying precursors and see how I go though.

good on you mate

author: @Plum_Daddy
text: can this be used as an active lasing medium? I kind of want to explore that front.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: dunno - i'm not an expert on this mate - it just came from something else i was doing

replies.author: @Plum_Daddy
replies.text: well to light up like that from just that one laser. that's an impressive threshhold. usually you need crazy powerful light sources to get that much and to mimic the laser's color like that is something you don't get out of most laser media.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Mighty Tighty Whitey And I'm Smuggling Plums i didn't know that mate - basically I am a chemist - though i did know they use thiourea zinc acetate for optical comms and this crystal is related to that one - anyway you clearly know more about this than me

replies.author: @Plum_Daddy
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
hey I don't really know that much. I've just started researching. I was going to use some rhodamine I extracted from a marker suspended in clear plastic but I've never seen a pump source that small do that.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dye_laser#mediaviewer/File:Rhodamine6G_Chloride.JPG
what you would expect from marker ink with a similar pump beam.

author: @odoniv
text:
There ya go!
Let me see, Red, Green, and Blue lasers....
Spread throughout some aero gel, and we have 3D television!!
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aerogel
And here is a display of a one color hologram:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player_embedded&v=G10bzatpuFc
Your crystals could enable color and motion.
Never thought I'd see it in my lifetime.
:-)

author: @MatthewLong8
text: Did it appear to you that the green illuminated the crystal more evenly? I wonder what blue would do. In other words I'm curious if the effect is linear or band limited.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i think the green was better - i would expect UV to be even better as it is a complexed metallo-organic salt crystal very similar to thiourea zinc acetate which has these properties and has been studied for applications in optical communications
